title: What Ratchet Does
text: Ratchet is Anveil Labs' incremental parsing engine, embedded inside the Anveil language-server framework since 2019 and now powering syntax analysis for editor plugins across roughly three dozen language grammars. Rather than reparsing an entire source file from scratch on every keystroke, Ratchet maintains a persistent syntax tree across edits and updates only the portion of the tree actually affected by a given change, reusing every unaffected subtree unchanged. This incremental approach is what makes Ratchet usable for live, as-you-type diagnostics and syntax highlighting even on large files, where a full reparse on every keystroke would introduce enough latency to make typing feel sluggish. Ratchet's tree representation is designed specifically to make this kind of reuse cheap: every node stores its own byte span and a content hash, so an editor's edit event can be mapped directly onto the specific tree region it invalidates without walking the entire tree to find it.

title: Node Reuse and Latency
text: Ratchet's incremental reparser reuses at least 99.2 percent of existing syntax-tree nodes by count for a typical single-character insertion or deletion edit, measured across a corpus of 50,000 real-world edit events captured from Anveil's own editor telemetry. Median keystroke-to-updated-tree latency measures 1.8 milliseconds across that same corpus, with a 99th-percentile latency of 14 milliseconds on files under 5,000 lines. A full, non-incremental reparse of the same median file takes 41 milliseconds, roughly 23 times slower than Ratchet's incremental update for an equivalent single-character edit. Ratchet's tree nodes average 48 bytes each in memory, including the content hash and byte-span fields used for reuse detection.

title: Tree Splicing and Retry
text: When an edit arrives, Ratchet first identifies the smallest existing tree node whose byte span fully contains the edited region, then re-lexes and re-parses only that node's own text rather than the entire file. If the resulting subtree's shape and boundary tokens match what the surrounding tree already expects, Ratchet splices the new subtree directly into the existing tree and reuses every other node completely unchanged, without re-examining sibling or ancestor nodes at all. When the edit changes the node's boundary in a way the parent doesn't expect, for example turning a single statement into two, Ratchet walks one level up the tree and retries parsing from the parent instead, repeating this upward walk only as far as actually necessary to produce a structurally consistent tree. This localized retry strategy is why Ratchet's average reparse cost tracks the size of the edited region rather than the size of the whole file, even for edits nested deep inside a large function body.

title: Latency by File Size
text: Reparse latency scales primarily with edit locality rather than file size for Ratchet's typical incremental path: a single-character edit inside a 200-line file completes in a median 1.6 milliseconds, while the same single-character edit inside a 20,000-line file completes in a median 2.1 milliseconds, a difference of well under a millisecond despite the hundredfold difference in file size. Structural edits that require an upward retry, such as adding a closing brace that changes a block's boundary, average 6.4 milliseconds regardless of file size, since the retry walk typically terminates within two or three tree levels rather than reaching the file's root. Only 0.4 percent of captured edit events in Anveil's telemetry corpus required a retry walk reaching all the way to the file's root node.

title: Latency by Edit Type
text:
Reparse latency varies substantially by edit type, since some edits invalidate only a single leaf token while others force a wider structural retry, as the following measurements show.

| Edit type | Nodes reused | Median latency | P99 latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single character | 99.2% | 1.8 ms | 14 ms |
| Line insertion | 96.1% | 3.2 ms | 22 ms |
| Bracket/brace change | 71.4% | 6.4 ms | 48 ms |

Bracket and brace changes reuse noticeably fewer nodes because they frequently shift block boundaries, forcing Ratchet's upward retry walk to re-parse a wider enclosing region than a purely local edit requires.

title: Error Recovery
text: Source code being actively edited is syntactically invalid for a meaningful fraction of its lifetime, since a developer mid-sentence, so to speak, has usually typed an opening bracket without its matching close, or half of a new statement. Ratchet's error-recovery layer inserts a designated error node into the tree at the point parsing fails, then attempts to resynchronize by scanning forward for a token that plausibly begins a new, valid construct, such as a statement keyword or a closing delimiter matching an open one still on the parser's stack. This resynchronization deliberately favors producing a mostly-correct tree over the smallest surrounding region quickly, even if the specific error node's exact boundaries turn out to be imprecise, since editor features like syntax highlighting and outline views depend on having a usable tree for the rest of the file even while one specific region remains invalid.

title: Error Recovery Quality
text: Ratchet's error-recovery resynchronization successfully locates a valid restart point within 3 tokens of the actual syntax error in 87 percent of captured invalid-syntax edit events, and within 10 tokens in 97 percent of events, based on a labeled sample of 8,200 invalid-syntax snapshots drawn from Anveil's telemetry corpus. The remaining 3 percent of events required falling back to whole-statement-level resynchronization, adding a median 4.1 milliseconds of additional reparse time compared to token-level recovery. Editor features depending on tree validity outside the immediate error region, such as bracket matching and code folding, remained correct in 99.6 percent of sampled invalid-syntax snapshots despite the presence of an active syntax error elsewhere in the file.

title: Keystroke-to-Tree Pipeline
text:
A single keystroke travels through the following pipeline stages inside Ratchet before an editor's syntax highlighting and diagnostics update. 1. The editor's edit-tracking layer computes the byte-range delta for the keystroke and forwards it to Ratchet.
2. Ratchet locates the smallest existing tree node whose span contains the edited range.
3. The affected region is re-lexed, producing an updated token stream for just that region.
4. The affected region is re-parsed against the surrounding grammar context, producing a candidate replacement subtree.
5. Ratchet splices the replacement subtree into the existing tree, or retries one level up if boundaries don't match. The full pipeline typically completes well within a single editor frame interval, keeping syntax highlighting visually synchronized with typing.

title: Prior Research: Boundary-Matching Reuse
text: Incremental parsing for editor tooling has an established research tradition beyond the English-language compiler-construction literature most language-server implementers cite first. Kowalski and Nowak's 2015 paper, Przyrostowa analiza składniowa w edytorach kodu źródłowego czasu rzeczywistego (Incremental Syntactic Analysis in Real-Time Source-Code Editors), formalized the node-reuse boundary-matching approach Ratchet's splicing step implements, evaluating reuse rates across several grammar families before concluding that boundary-token matching alone, without a full subtree equality check, was sufficient for the great majority of real-world edits. Anveil's early prototype of Ratchet's splicing logic drew directly on this boundary-matching formulation rather than the more computationally expensive full-tree-diff approach some contemporaneous research explored.

title: Grammar Coverage
text: Ratchet ships grammars for 38 languages as of its most recent release, ranging from statically typed systems languages to markup and configuration formats, each grammar implemented as a standalone, independently versioned module loaded on demand by the host editor. The three most heavily used grammars in Anveil's aggregated telemetry, covering roughly 61 percent of all tracked edit events, are its JavaScript, Python, and Java grammars respectively. Adding a new grammar to Ratchet's supported set takes a median of 11 engineer-weeks from initial grammar authoring to passing Anveil's full incremental-reparse conformance suite, based on the six most recently added grammars.

title: Benchmark Comparison
text:
Anveil benchmarked Ratchet against two widely used open-source incremental-parsing libraries on an identical single-character-edit workload across a shared 400-file corpus.

| Parser | Median latency | Node reuse rate | Memory per 1000 lines |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ratchet | 1.8 ms | 99.2% | 340 KB |
| Library A | 2.4 ms | 97.8% | 410 KB |
| Library B | 3.1 ms | 94.6% | 290 KB |

Ratchet's lead on both latency and reuse rate comes primarily from its byte-span-and-hash-based node identification, which avoids the deeper structural equality checks the two comparison libraries perform during splicing.

title: Incremental Reparse Complexity
text: Ratchet's incremental reparse cost follows a bound of O(d + k), where d is the depth of the upward retry walk from the initially affected node to the point where boundaries stabilize, and k is the size, in tokens, of the region actually re-lexed and re-parsed at each retry level. This bound holds because Ratchet never re-examines a sibling subtree it has already determined to be unaffected, in contrast to a naive re-diffing approach that would cost O(n) in the size of the whole file for every edit regardless of locality. In the overwhelming majority of captured edits, d stays at 0 or 1, meaning effective cost tracks k alone, the size of the immediately edited region, which is why Ratchet's measured latency in practice correlates far more strongly with edit locality than with total file size, matching the dense reparse-latency measurements reported elsewhere in this specification.

title: Grammar-Authoring Checklist
text:
Engineers adding a new language grammar to Ratchet follow this checklist before the grammar is accepted into the supported-languages set. 1. Author the grammar definition using Ratchet's declarative grammar format, covering the language's full published syntax specification.
2. Generate the parser and lexer tables from the grammar definition using Ratchet's build tooling.
3. Run the grammar against Anveil's corpus of open-source repositories in the target language and record any parse failures.
4. Author error-recovery resynchronization rules for the language's most common syntax-error patterns, informed by the corpus run.
5. Pass the full incremental-reparse conformance suite, including node-reuse-rate and latency thresholds, before merging. Grammars failing step three at a rate above 0.5 percent of files are returned to authoring rather than proceeding to error-recovery work.

title: Prior Research: Error-Recovery Strategy
text: Error-recovery strategy for real-time parsers is likewise informed by research published outside English-language venues. Willems and de Vries's 2017 study, Foutherstel in incrementele parsers voor interactieve ontwikkelomgevingen (Error Recovery in Incremental Parsers for Interactive Development Environments), compared token-level resynchronization against statement-level resynchronization across several grammar families, finding token-level recovery located a plausible restart point faster in most cases but occasionally produced a less semantically sensible tree than the slower statement-level approach. Ratchet's fallback to statement-level resynchronization after a bounded number of failed token-level attempts, described in this specification's error-recovery section, directly reflects the hybrid strategy this study recommended rather than committing exclusively to either approach.

title: Memory Footprint
text: A fully parsed Ratchet syntax tree for a typical 1,000-line source file consumes approximately 340 kilobytes of resident memory, including node metadata, byte spans, and content hashes used for incremental reuse. Anveil's language-server process, hosting Ratchet alongside several other analysis subsystems, reports a median steady-state memory footprint of 180 megabytes across roughly 60 simultaneously open files in a typical large-project editing session. Tree memory usage grows sublinearly with file count in practice, since Ratchet shares grammar tables and lexer state across all open files of the same language rather than duplicating them per file, saving a measured 22 megabytes on a session with 60 open JavaScript files compared to a naive per-file allocation baseline.

title: Spec Addendum: Node-Reuse Methodology
text: Specification addendum, Ratchet incremental-reparse conformance suite, section 4.2. Clarifies the measurement methodology behind the headline node-reuse figure cited elsewhere in this specification: Ratchet's incremental reparser reuses at least 98.5 percent of existing syntax-tree nodes by count for a typical single-character insertion or deletion edit, measured across a corpus of 50,000 real-world edit events captured from Anveil's own editor telemetry. This addendum exists because early conformance-suite reports rounded the figure inconsistently across different grammar families, and this section now specifies the aggregate figure across all 38 supported grammars combined rather than any single grammar in isolation.

title: Audit Finding: Error-Recovery Regression
text: Internal audit finding, Anveil quality engineering, Q3 2024 review. The audit re-sampled Ratchet's error-recovery telemetry independently of the original conformance-suite measurement process to confirm the published figures remained accurate after several grammar updates. The audit's independent sample found that Ratchet's error-recovery resynchronization successfully locates a valid restart point within 3 tokens of the actual syntax error in 79 percent of captured invalid-syntax edit events, and within 10 tokens in 97 percent of events, based on a labeled sample of 8,200 invalid-syntax snapshots drawn from Anveil's telemetry corpus. The audit flagged the 3-token figure as a modest but real regression against the previously published number, tracing the gap primarily to grammar updates for two languages whose resynchronization rules had not been revisited since their original authoring. The audit recommended a scheduled quarterly re-sampling process rather than treating the original measurement as permanently valid.

title: History: From Whole-File Reparse to Local Splicing
text: Anveil Labs began Ratchet in 2018 as an internal tool for a single editor product, without any ambition at the time to support incremental reparsing at all; the earliest prototype simply reparsed the entire file on every keystroke and relied on debouncing to keep the editor responsive, an approach that worked adequately only because the earliest supported grammars were small and the target files rarely exceeded a few hundred lines. That debounced full-reparse approach began breaking down almost immediately once Anveil's editor gained traction among users working on larger codebases, where even a debounced reparse of a multi-thousand-line file introduced a visible, distracting delay between typing and updated syntax highlighting. The team's first serious incremental-parsing prototype, built in early 2019, attempted a full tree-diffing approach that compared the entire old and new trees node by node to identify reusable subtrees, an approach that correctly identified reuse opportunities but whose own diffing cost scaled with file size closely enough to erode much of the latency benefit incremental parsing was meant to provide. This diffing-cost problem directly motivated the boundary-token-matching approach Ratchet ships today, informed by external research the team encountered while investigating why full tree-diffing wasn't delivering the latency improvement they expected, and which let the engine identify reuse opportunities locally, at the edited node, without ever comparing the rest of the tree at all. Error recovery was not part of Ratchet's original design; the earliest versions simply failed to produce any tree at all for a file containing invalid syntax, which proved unworkable the moment real users, whose files are invalid syntax for a meaningful fraction of every editing session, started relying on the tool. The token-level-then-statement-level hybrid resynchronization strategy Ratchet uses today was developed iteratively across 2019 and 2020, arriving at its current form only after the team's initial token-only approach was found to occasionally produce trees that were syntactically valid but semantically nonsensical in ways that confused downstream editor features more than an explicit error node would have. Grammar support expanded gradually rather than all at once, starting with the four languages Anveil's original editor product needed and growing to the current 38 only as external teams began adopting Ratchet as a standalone library separate from Anveil's own editor. Looking back, the engineering team describes the move from whole-tree diffing to local boundary-matching as the single most consequential architectural decision in Ratchet's history, since it is what actually made sub-frame-latency incremental reparsing achievable rather than merely theoretically faster than a full reparse.

title: Incident Postmortem: The May 2023 Nested-Array Freeze
text: In May 2023, users of a popular open-source code editor built on Ratchet began reporting the editor becoming unresponsive for several seconds at a time while editing large, deeply nested JSON configuration files, a symptom initially attributed to the editor's own rendering layer rather than to Ratchet's incremental reparser. Investigation eventually traced the freezes to a regression introduced in a JSON grammar update shipped three weeks earlier, which had changed how the grammar represented deeply nested array literals in a way that interacted badly with Ratchet's upward-retry logic for boundary mismatches. Specifically, an edit near the bottom of a deeply nested array now frequently produced a boundary mismatch that propagated upward through every enclosing array level rather than stabilizing after one or two levels as the retry logic was designed to expect, so a single keystroke in a file with 200 levels of nesting could trigger roughly 200 sequential retry attempts before producing a final tree. Files with this pattern were uncommon in Anveil's original conformance-suite corpus, which is why the regression passed conformance testing despite its severe effect on the specific file shapes that did trigger it; the suite's JSON test files rarely exceeded 15 levels of nesting, well short of where the retry cost became noticeable. The fix, shipped as an emergency patch six days after the first user reports, added an explicit depth cap to the JSON grammar's retry propagation, falling back to a wider single-pass reparse of the entire affected top-level value once retry depth exceeded a threshold, trading a larger but bounded reparse cost for eliminating the unbounded retry chain entirely. Anveil's post-incident review concluded the core incremental-reparse architecture was not at fault; the regression was specific to one grammar's boundary-matching rules rather than to Ratchet's general splicing logic, which behaved correctly for every other supported grammar throughout the incident. The review's primary recommendation was expanding the conformance suite's corpus to include deliberately deep, pathological nesting patterns for every grammar rather than relying on organically collected real-world files, which tend to under-represent unusual-but-valid structures precisely because such files are individually rare even though the risk they expose is systemic. A secondary recommendation proposed a general per-edit retry-depth cap inside Ratchet's core splicing logic itself, rather than requiring each grammar to implement its own cap independently, a change now shipped in Ratchet's current release and applied uniformly across all 38 supported grammars regardless of individual grammar authors having considered the failure mode themselves.
